data.title: Institutional Exit Puts Wabtec Freight Exposure And Valuation In Spotlight
data.description:
• A prominent institutional fund has fully exited its position in Westinghouse Air Brake Technologies (NYSE:WAB), closing its stake recently.
  • The fund cited concerns that potential consolidation among Class 1 railroads could affect Wabtec’s core freight operations and future profitability.
  • This move draws attention to how a smaller group of large customers might influence pricing, contract terms, and order patterns for freight equipment and services.

Westinghouse Air Brake Technologies, commonly known as Wabtec, supplies equipment, systems, and services to freight and passenger rail operators, with Class 1 railroads a key part of its customer base. Recent headlines around acquisitions and a solid order backlog have focused attention on growth opportunities. At the same time, this divestment points investors toward concentration risks that come with serving a limited pool of large freight customers. As rail operators weigh potential mergers or alliances, questions around project timing, capital spending decisions, and tender structures become more important to monitor.

For you as an investor, the main issue is how any structural shift among Class 1 railroads might affect Wabtec’s revenue mix, product margins, and bargaining power over the medium term. The fund’s exit does not, by itself, signal a specific outcome, but it does highlight scenarios worth stress testing, such as fewer large buyers with stronger leverage, changes in standardization of equipment, and different maintenance or retrofit cycles that could influence Wabtec’s freight related earnings profile.

data.title: Is ARCHION (TSE:543A) Share Weakness Creating A Potential Value Opportunity?
data.description:
• Wondering whether ARCHION at ¥398 still offers value, or if the recent share price leaves little on the table? This article breaks down what the current price could imply.
  • The stock has been volatile recently, with a 23.3% 7 day decline, a 1.7% 30 day decline and a 2.1% decline over the past year, on top of a 27.6% 3 year and 57.1% 5 year decline.
  • That kind of price performance often follows shifts in sentiment, changes in industry conditions, or company specific developments that reset expectations. While this article does not focus on a single event, it treats these moves as a sign that investors are reassessing what ARCHION might be worth over the long term.
  • Even after those moves, ARCHION currently holds a valuation score of 5 out of 6 on Simply Wall St's checks for undervaluation. The sections that follow will compare different valuation approaches before circling back to a fuller way of thinking about what this score really means for you.

Find out why ARCHION's -2.1% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: ARCHION Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today. It is essentially asking what all of ARCHION’s expected future cash flows are worth in today’s money.

For ARCHION, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in ¥. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of ¥73.5m, so the valuation leans heavily on future estimates rather than recent cash generation. Analyst input is available for the earlier years; then Simply Wall St extrapolates out to year ten, with projected free cash flow in the 10 year view remaining in the hundreds of millions of ¥ each year.

Bringing those projected cash flows back to today using a discount rate produces an estimated intrinsic value of ¥2,989.82 per share. Against the current share price of ¥398, the DCF output implies an intrinsic discount of 86.7%, which indicates that the shares are trading well below this model’s estimate of value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests ARCHION is undervalued by 86.7%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 18 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for ARCHION.

Approach 2: ARCHION Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for how much you are paying for each unit of earnings. It links directly to what matters most for shareholders, the company’s ability to generate profit relative to its share price.

What counts as a “normal” P/E tends to reflect how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk is often associated with higher P/E ratios, while slower growth or higher risk usually lines up with lower P/E levels.

ARCHION currently trades on a P/E of 2.92x. That is well below the Machinery industry average of 14.73x and also below the peer average of 13.41x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for ARCHION is 9.34x, which represents the P/E that its model suggests could be appropriate given factors such as earnings growth characteristics, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks.

The Fair Ratio goes a step further than simple peer or industry comparisons because it blends these qualitative and quantitative elements into one number, rather than assuming that all Machinery companies should trade on the same multiple.

Comparing ARCHION’s actual P/E of 2.92x with the Fair Ratio of 9.34x suggests that the shares are trading below this model based reference point.

Result: UNDERVALUED

A Narrative links what you believe about the business to a financial forecast, then connects that forecast to a fair value, so you can see how your view translates into a price per share.

  • Over the last 12 months, Oberbank converted €885.8 million of revenue into €352.3 million of net income, which works out to a 39.8% net profit margin compared with 43.2% in the prior year.

However, the most recent year saw earnings soften and margins move from 43.2% to 39.8%, so anyone leaning bullish needs to factor in both the strong multi year track record and the more recent step down in profitability.

  • The shares trade on a trailing P/E of 16x versus a peer average of 12.3x and a wider European Banks industry average of 11x, while the current share price of €80 sits modestly below the DCF fair value estimate of €83.91.

Approach 1: Marathon Petroleum Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today’s dollars. It focuses on what you are ultimately paying for, the cash the business may generate over time.

For Marathon Petroleum, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on last twelve months free cash flow of about $5.76b. Analyst projections and Simply Wall St extrapolations suggest free cash flow figures such as $7.55b in 2026 and $5.94b in 2030, all expressed in $. These values are discounted using the model’s assumptions to reflect their value in today’s terms.

Pulling this together, the DCF output points to an estimated intrinsic value of about $404 per share, compared with the recent share price of $222.62. That gap implies an intrinsic discount of 44.9%, which indicates the shares are currently assessed as undervalued under this specific model and its inputs.
